Rate Limits & Scaling in the CCR Improvement Loop

A ground-up walkthrough of where GitHub API pressure comes from, how you get throttled, and the levers to scale.

The one spine: a deterministic prep stage fetches every merged PR before any AI agent runs. That fetch path is what burns GitHub API budget — so every scaling question reduces to: where do its calls come from, how is it throttled, and how do you raise or spread the load?

The 11 steps

  1. What this is & why limits bite
  2. The 4 domain nouns
  3. The external contract
  4. The function that does the work
  5. Walking fetchPrToCache
  6. The concurrency guard
  7. The invariant everything relies on
  8. The four scaling levers
  9. Shipped vs deferred
  10. Two proposed wins (#6 & #7)
  11. Verify against the live system
  12. Where a change would land

STEP 1 What this thing is & why rate limits bite

If you don't know where the load lives, you'll tune the wrong knob.

The CCR (Copilot Code Review) improvement loop mines merged pull requests to learn review patterns. Before any AI agent runs, a deterministic prep stage calls the GitHub REST + GraphQL APIs — one burst of calls per PR. At real volume (about 280–330 PRs/month for a single busy repo), it is this prep stage — not the agent — that hits GitHub's ceiling and fails the run. Everything in this guide is about that one stage.

STEP 2 Internalize the 4 domain nouns

These four words explain every 403 you'll ever see here.

Read: api-rate-limit.md §2–§3

  • Primary limit — an hourly budget that refills each hour. Exceed it → 403 with X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0. REST and GraphQL have separate pools.
  • Secondary limit — an anti-abuse governor on how you call (too many concurrent, too many per minute). Independent of the budget; often returns a Retry-After header.
  • The authenticating token — which token you use sets the primary ceiling (see table in Step 8).
  • Endpoint invocation vs HTTP request — every call uses gh api --paginate, so one "call" can be many HTTP requests. Cost estimates are lower bounds.
Key trap: GET /rate_limit only reports the primary budget. It can read "plenty remaining" while a secondary limit is actively throttling you.
Mental check: why can /rate_limit say "plenty left" while you still get 403s?

Because it only reports the primary hourly budget. A secondary (burst/concurrency) governor throttles you independently and is never shown by that endpoint.

STEP 3 The external contract — how load is triggered

These flags are the only human-facing knobs that control how much budget a run spends.

Open: scripts/fetch-prs.ts — the usage() block (~L44–L66)

The CLI flags --min-prs, --limit, --concurrency, and --refresh, plus the workflow dispatch input max_prs, are the entire surface for controlling API load. A run with max_prs = 0 (uncapped) fetches the full cohort — that's the mode that blows the budget.

STEP 4 The one function that does the real work

Every rate-limited call in the whole system originates in this one function.

scripts/fetch-prs.ts → fetchPrToCache (~L362–L410)

Its job in one sentence: fetch every REST + GraphQL payload for a single PR and write an immutable cache file pr-{n}.json.

STEP 5 Walking fetchPrToCache in 3 phases

Seeing the phases tells you exactly which term you can shrink.

Phase 1 — Cache gate (~L367–L372): if pr-{n}.json exists and no --refresh, return immediately. Zero API cost.
Phase 2 — Fixed per-PR fan-out (~L379–L387): 5 REST calls (pulls/{n}, reviews, comments, issues/{n}/comments, commits) + 1 GraphQL thread call, under one Promise.all.
Phase 3 — Per-commit fan-out (~L392–L411): one commits/{sha} call per commit, parallel and order-preserving. This N term dominates large PRs.
cost ≈ 6 + N   endpoint invocations per PR   (N = number of commits)
Mental check: which phase costs nothing, and why does that matter later?

Phase 1 (the cache gate). Because a re-run reuses cached PRs for free, backfill can be paused and resumed with zero rework — this is the foundation of Step 7 & Step 8's Lever 3.

STEP 6 The concurrency guard that tames secondary limits

Without it, parallel fan-out would make secondary throttling worse, not better.

scripts/utils.ts → Semaphore class + ghRequestLimiter (~L195–L269)

Every async gh call in Step 5 is admitted through a single shared counting semaphore (default 8, override with CCR_GH_MAX_CONCURRENCY). So workers × per-PR fan-out can never exceed the ceiling, keeping bursts well under GitHub's ~100-concurrent guidance. Retries also honor the server's Retry-After header via parseRetryAfterMs.

STEP 7 The non-obvious invariant everything relies on

This single property is what makes scaling to any repo count possible.

Read: api-rate-limit.md §4, "Lever 3"

The cache file is immutable and fetch is idempotent (Step 5, Phase 1). A run can check GET /rate_limit, work until the budget is low, re-dispatch itself next hour, and resume from cache with zero rework. The cache is the enabler beneath every "spread the work" strategy.

STEP 8 The four scaling levers

In leverage order — auth first, orchestration last.

Read: api-rate-limit.md §3 (auth table) + §4 (levers)

Auth methodPrimary REST limit
GITHUB_TOKEN (default Actions, personal repo)1,000 / hr
GITHUB_TOKEN on GitHub Enterprise Cloud15,000 / hr
GitHub App installation5,000 / hr
Personal Access Token5,000 / hr
  • Lever 1 — Raise the ceiling (auth): swap GITHUB_TOKEN for an App token / PAT. 5–15× budget, no fetch-logic change. Highest leverage.
  • Lever 2 — Cut calls/PR: shrink the N term (deferred #3) or move reads onto GraphQL's separate pool (deferred #4). Two proposed structural wins — see Step 9a — go further: source per-commit detail from local git (removes N from the budget entirely) and batch fixed calls via GraphQL aliasing (moves them to the separate point pool).
  • Lever 3 — Spread the work: weekly windows + a /rate_limit-aware resumable loop riding the cache.
  • Lever 4 — Orchestrate: a central pacer draining a repos × months matrix, one window/hour.
Why auth is #1: on a personal workflow repo the 1,000/hr ceiling is the binding constraint — runs fail around 70 PRs (~1,000 calls). Nothing else you do matters until that ceiling moves.
Mental check: why doesn't raising the auth tier fix burst 403s?

Auth only raises the primary budget. Secondary (burst/concurrency) limits are never relaxed by a higher tier — that's why the semaphore in Step 6 stays essential at every tier.

STEP 9 What's shipped vs deferred

Tells you what's safe to build on and what still has open design questions.

Read: optimize-api-call-plan.md — "Sequencing — STATUS"

  • Shipped #1: deleted dead commits/{sha}/pulls call → cost 6+2N → 6+N.
  • Shipped #5: the limiter + Retry-After-aware backoff.
  • Shipped #2: bounded parallel fan-out (built on #5's limiter).
  • Deferred #3: selective commit-detail — blocked by a distinctFiles coupling.
  • Deferred #4: full GraphQL migration — partial win, pagination caveats.

STEP 9a Two proposed structural wins (#6 & #7)

These attack the primary-REST ceiling that #1–#5 all left loaded — the one that actually fails runs.

Read: optimize-api-call-plan.md §6 & §7

#6 — Local-git commit detail. Every field the per-commit commits/{sha} call writes (sha, committedAt, files, patches) is a native git concept, obtainable from a local clone. Git-protocol operations don't draw from the REST or GraphQL pools, so this doesn't shrink N — it removes N from the rate-limited path entirely, leaving ≈ 6 + 0. This is the fix #3/#4 couldn't reach: both were blocked specifically on patches, which git yields natively.
#7 — GraphQL-batched fixed calls. GraphQL aliasing puts multiple PRs in one HTTP request (p0: …, p1: …), moving the fixed 6 onto GraphQL's separate ~5,000-point/hr pool and cutting HTTP request count — direct secondary-limit relief. It still can't touch N (no patch text in GraphQL); that's #6's job.
Composed: #6 removes N, #7 moves the fixed 6 off REST → per-PR cost on the primary REST budget drops toward near zero. A qualitative shift beyond #1–#5.
Caveats: #6 needs a blobless partial clone + fetch-depth: 0 and git-vs-REST field-equivalence tests; #7 still paginates connections and must stay under ghRequestLimiter. Neither replaces the thread isResolved / linked-issues GraphQL call — that's review-model state, absent from git.
Mental check: why can local git succeed where the deferred GraphQL migration (#4) could not?

Both #3 and #4 were blocked on per-commit patch text. GraphQL doesn't expose patches; git produces them natively — and git traffic is metered on a separate, generous transport budget, not the REST/GraphQL pools.

STEP 10 Verify the model against the live system

A read-only measurement proves whether the 6+N model matches reality.

The rate_limit endpoint is free and read-only. Sample it before and after a small capped fetch:

gh api rate_limit | jq '.resources.core'
# then a capped run:
node scripts/fetch-prs.ts --repo Azure/azure-sdk-for-python --min-prs 5
# then re-run the first line

Matches my model: core.remaining drops by roughly 5 × (6 + avg N), scaled up by the pagination factor. If it drops far more, pagination/retries are inflating the count — exactly why the docs call 6+N a lower bound.

STEP 11 Where a scaling change would land

Two concrete spots, depending on which lever you pull.

  • Raise the ceiling: swap GH_TOKEN in .github/workflows/ccr-improvement-loop.md — no code change.
  • Shrink N / add resumability: the per-commit loop in scripts/fetch-prs.ts (~L392–L411), plus a new /rate_limit-aware dispatch wrapper around the run.

Check your model

Pick an answer for each question, then submit. Correct answers and explanations reveal automatically.

1. At real volume, which part of the loop actually hits GitHub's rate limit?
The prep stage (fetchPrToCache) issues all the API calls; the agent runs afterward on cached data. (Step 1 & 4)
2. Why can GET /rate_limit show budget remaining while you're still being throttled?
Secondary limits govern how you call (concurrency/bursts) and are invisible to /rate_limit. (Step 2)
3. In the cost model 6 + N, what is N?
Phase 3 issues one commits/{sha} call per commit — the dominant term on large PRs. (Step 5)
4. What lets a killed backfill resume without re-spending budget?
Cached pr-{n}.json files are reused for free, so re-dispatch resumes with zero rework. (Step 7)
5. Which lever gives the biggest immediate gain, and why isn't it enough alone?
Auth (Lever 1) is a 5–15× jump on the primary budget, but the semaphore stays essential because secondary limits are unchanged at any tier. (Steps 6 & 8)
6. Why can proposed item #6 (local git) remove the N term when deferred #3 and #4 could not?
#3/#4 were both blocked on per-commit patch text; GraphQL can't expose it, but git produces it locally on a separate transport budget. (Step 9a)
Sources in this folder: api-rate-limit.md, optimize-api-call-plan.md, scripts/fetch-prs.ts, scripts/utils.ts.
